The heap is where your object data is stored. This is what the garbage collector manages.
Most performance tuning focuses on the heap size and which garbage collector you choose.
The Three Components That Affect Performance
When tuning Java performance, you focus on three things:
1. The Heap
Where all your objects live. Divided into generations:
// When you create objects
List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();  // ArrayList object вҶ’ Heap
names.add("Alice");                      // String object вҶ’ Heap  
names.add("Bob");                        // String object вҶ’ Heap
// All these objects allocated in Young Generation (Eden)
2. The Garbage Collector
Automatically reclaims memory from dead objects. Chosen at JVM startup:
java -XX:+UseG1GC MyApp          # Use G1 collector
java -XX:+UseSerialGC MyApp      # Use Serial collector
java -XX:+UseParallelGC MyApp    # Use Parallel collector
3. The JIT Compiler
Compiles frequently-executed bytecode to native machine code for speed. Usually, it doesnвҖҷt
need tuning in modern JVMs.
The focus: Most performance tuning is about the heap and the garbage collector.

Now letвҖҷs see what actually happens to your objects.
Step 1: New Objects Go to Eden
public void processOrders() {

After Minor GC:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

What happens:
1. JVM pauses all application threads
2. Scans Eden for objects still in use (referenced)
3. Copies live objects to Survivor Space (S0)
4. Deletes everything else (unreferenced objects)
5. Resumes application
Unreferenced objects are deleted instantly. No cleanup code needed.

Major GC triggered:
1. Stop all application threads (LONG pause)
2. Mark all reachable objects in entire heap
3. Sweep unreachable objects
4. Compact memory (move objects together)
5. Resume application
Major GC is expensive. Pause times can be 100ms to several seconds, depending on heap
size.

The JVM offers different collectors for different needs.
